text: In the domain of high-dimensional credit card fraud detection, feature selection is a critical step for optimizing model performance and interpretability. This study compares two prevalent feature selection strategies: the model’s native feature importance rankings and those derived from SHAP (SHapley Additive exPlanations) values. We evaluate these methods using five distinct classifiers—XGBoost, Decision Tree, CatBoost, Extremely Randomized Trees, and Random Forest—using the Area under the Precision-Recall Curve (AUPRC) as the primary performance metric. Experiments conducted on the Kaggle Credit Card Fraud Detection dataset, supported by rigorous statistical testing, reveal that models utilizing built-in feature importance consistently outperform those using SHAP-based selection across various feature subset sizes. Given these findings, we recommend employing native importance rankings for large-scale datasets. This approach is not only more effective but also significantly more practical, as native rankings are generated during the training process without the additional computational overhead required by SHAP calculations. Consequently, prioritizing built-in importance offers a more efficient and streamlined workflow for developing complex fraud detection systems.
